text: In this video, we would like to present a house advancement flap anoplasty for severe posthermoredectomy anal stenosis. A seventy-two years old female patient presented at our clinic complaining of difficult defecation. She was unable to open her bowels without laxatives. Symptoms started after a hemoredectomy that was performed six years ago.
The patient is positioned in a prone jackknife position with the buttocks taped aside, revealing the anal region. The surgeon measures the anal opening, which is approximately five millimeters wide.
The surgeon performs a horizontal incision on the scar tissue, extending the incision up to the level of the dentate line.
The surgeon draws a skin flap on the anal region, ensuring that the flap's blood supply is not compromised during preparation.
The surgeon stitches the skin flap to the anus using three interrupted sutures with 3-0 Vicryl, then closes the skin defect with the same type of sutures.
The post-operative image shows the patient's anal opening, which has increased to approximately twenty millimeters, two weeks after the house advancement flap anoplasty.
